title: Iseult Of Brittany
content:
آ آ آ آ A year had flown, and oï؟½er the sea away,
آ آ آ آ In Cornwall, Tristram and Queen Iseult lay;
آ آ آ آ In King Marcï؟½s chapel, in Tyntagel old 
آ آ آ آ There in a ship they bore those lovers cold.
آ آ آ آ The young surviving Iseult, one bright day,
آ آ آ آ Had wanderï؟½d forth. Her children were at play
آ آ آ آ In a green circular hollow in the heath
آ آ آ آ Which borders the sea-shore a country path
آ آ آ آ Creeps over it from the tillï؟½d fields behind.
آ آ آ آ The hollowï؟½s grassy banks are soft-inclined,
آ آ آ آ And to one standing on them, far and near
آ آ آ آ The lone unbroken view spreads bright and clear
آ آ آ آ Over the waste. This cirque of open ground
آ آ آ آ Is light and green; the heather, which all round
آ آ آ آ Creeps thickly, grows not here; but the pale grass
آ آ آ آ Is strewn with rocks, and many a shiverï؟½d mass
آ آ آ آ Of veinï؟½d white-gleaming quartz, and here and there
آ آ آ آ Dotted with holly-trees and juniper.
آ آ آ آ In the smooth centre of the opening stood
آ آ آ آ Three hollies side by side, and made a screen,
آ آ آ آ Warm with the winter-sun, of burnishï؟½d green
آ آ آ آ With scarlet berries gemmï؟½d, the fell-fareï؟½s food.
آ آ آ آ Under the glittering hollies Iseult stands,
آ آ آ آ Watching her children play; their little hands
آ آ آ آ Are busy gathering spars of quartz, and streams
آ آ آ آ Of stagshorn for their hats; anon, with screams
آ آ آ آ Of mad delight they drop their spoils, and bound
آ آ آ آ Among the holly-clumps and broken ground,
آ آ آ آ Racing full speed, and startling in their rush
آ آ آ آ The fell-fares and the speckled missel-thrush
آ آ آ آ Out of their glossy coverts; but when now
آ آ آ آ Their cheeks were flushï؟½d, and over each hot brow,
آ آ آ آ Under the featherï؟½d hats of the sweet pair,
آ آ آ آ In blinding masses showerï؟½d the golden hair 
آ آ آ آ Then Iseult callï؟½d them to her, and the three
آ آ آ آ Clusterï؟½d under the holly-screen, and she
آ آ آ آ Told them an old-world Breton history.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ Warm in their mantles wrapt the three stood there,
آ آ آ آ Under the hollies, in the clear still air 
آ آ آ آ Mantles with those rich furs deep glistering
آ آ آ آ Which Venice ships do from swart Egypt bring.
آ آ آ آ Long they stayï؟½d still then, pacing at their ease,
آ آ آ آ Moved up and down under the glossy trees.
آ آ آ آ But still, as they pursued their warm dry road,
آ آ آ آ From Iseultï؟½s lips the unbroken story flowï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And still the children listenï؟½d, their blue eyes
آ آ آ آ Fixï؟½d on their motherï؟½s face in wide surprise;
آ آ آ آ Nor did their looks stray once to the sea-side,
آ آ آ آ Nor to the brown heaths round them, bright and wide,
آ آ آ آ Nor to the snow, which, though ï؟½twas all away
آ آ آ آ From the open heath, still by the hedgerows lay,
آ آ آ آ Nor to the shining sea-fowl, that with screams
آ آ آ آ Bore up from where the bright Atlantic gleams,
آ آ آ آ Swooping to landward; nor to where, quite clear,
آ آ آ آ The fell-fares settled on the thickets near.
آ آ آ آ And they would still have listenï؟½d, till dark night
آ آ آ آ Came keen and chill down on the heather bright;
آ آ آ آ But, when the red glow on the sea grew cold,
آ آ آ آ And the grey turrets of the castle old
آ آ آ آ Lookï؟½d sternly through the frosty evening-air,
آ آ آ آ Then Iseult took by the hand those children fair,
آ آ آ آ And brought her tale to an end, and found the path,
آ آ آ آ And led them home over the darkening heath.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ And is she happy? Does she see unmovï؟½d
آ آ آ آ The days in which she might have lived and loved
آ آ آ آ Slip without bringing bliss slowly away,
آ آ آ آ One after one, to-morrow like to-day?
آ آ آ آ Joy has not found her yet, nor ever will 
آ آ آ آ Is it this thought which makes her mien so still,
آ آ آ آ Her features so fatigued, her eyes, though sweet,
آ آ آ آ So sunk, so rarely lifted save to meet
آ آ آ آ Her childrenï؟½s? She moves slow; her voice alone
آ آ آ آ Hath yet an infantine and silver tone.
آ آ آ آ But even that comes languidly; in truth,
آ آ آ آ She seems one dying in a mask of youth.
آ آ آ آ And now she will go home, and softly lay
آ آ آ آ Her laughing children in their beds, and play
آ آ آ آ Awhile with them before they sleep; and then
آ آ آ آ Sheï؟½ll light her silver lamp, which fishermen
آ آ آ آ Dragging their nets through the rough waves, afar,
آ آ آ آ Along this iron coast, know like a star,
آ آ آ آ And take her broidery-frame, and there sheï؟½ll sit
آ آ آ آ Hour after hour, her gold curls sweeping it;
آ آ آ آ Lifting her soft-bent head only to mind
آ آ آ آ Her children, or to listen to the wind.
آ آ آ آ And when the clock peals midnight, she will move
آ آ آ آ Her work away, and let her fingers rove
آ آ آ آ Across the shaggy brows of Tristramï؟½s hound
آ آ آ آ Who lies, guarding her feet, along the ground;
آ آ آ آ Or else she will fall musing, her blue eyes
آ آ آ آ Fixt, her slight hands claspï؟½d on her lap; then rise,
آ آ آ آ And at her prie-dieu kneel, until she have told
آ آ آ آ Her rosary-beads of ebony tippï؟½d with gold,
آ آ آ آ Then to her soft sleep and to-morrowï؟½ll be
آ آ آ آ To-dayï؟½s exact repeated effigy.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ Yes, it is lonely for her in her hall.
آ آ آ آ The children, and the grey-hairï؟½d seneschal,
آ آ آ آ Her women, and Sir Tristramï؟½s aged hound,
آ آ آ آ Are there the sole companions to be found.
آ آ آ آ But these she loves; and noisier life than this
آ آ آ آ She would find ill to bear, weak as she is.
آ آ آ آ She has her children, too, and night and day
آ آ آ آ Is with them; and the wide heaths where they play,
آ آ آ آ The hollies, and the cliff, and the sea-shore,
آ آ آ آ The sand, the sea-birds, and the distant sails,
آ آ آ آ These are to her dear as to them; the tales
آ آ آ آ With which this day the children she beguiled
آ آ آ آ She gleaned from Breton grandames, when a child,
آ آ آ آ In every hut along this sea-coast wild.
آ آ آ آ She herself loves them still, and, when they are told,
آ آ آ آ Can forget all to hear them, as of old.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ Dear saints, it is not sorrow, as I hear,
آ آ آ آ Not suffering, which shuts up eye and ear
آ آ آ آ To all that has delighted them before,
آ آ آ آ And lets us be what we were once no more.
آ آ آ آ No, we may suffer deeply, yet retain
آ آ آ آ Power to be moved and soothed, for all our pain,
آ آ آ آ By what of old pleased us, and will again.
آ آ آ آ No, ï؟½tis the gradual furnace of the world,
آ آ آ آ In whose hot air our spirits are upcurlï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Until they crumble, or else grow like steel 
آ آ آ آ Which kills in us the bloom, the youth, the spring 
آ آ آ آ Which leaves the fierce necessity to feel,
آ آ آ آ But takes away the power this can avail,
آ آ آ آ By drying up our joy in everything,
آ آ آ آ To make our former pleasures all seem stale.
آ آ آ آ This, or some tyrannous single thought, some fit
آ آ آ آ Of passion, which subdues our souls to it,
آ آ آ آ Till for its sake alone we live and move 
آ آ آ آ Call it ambition, or remorse, or love 
آ آ آ آ This too can change us wholly, and make seem
آ آ آ آ All which we did before, shadow and dream.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ And yet, I swear, it angers me to see
آ آ آ آ How this fool passion gulls men potently;
آ آ آ آ Being, in truth, but a diseased unrest,
آ آ آ آ And an unnatural overheat at best.
آ آ آ آ How they are full of languor and distress
آ آ آ آ Not having it; which when they do possess,
آ آ آ آ They straightway are burnt up with fume and care,
آ آ آ آ And spend their lives in posting here and there
آ آ آ آ Where this plague drives them; and have little ease,
آ آ آ آ Are furious with themselves, and hard to please.
آ آ آ آ Like that bald Caesar, the famed Roman wight,
آ آ آ آ Who wept at reading of a Grecian knight
آ آ آ آ Who made a name at younger years than he;
آ آ آ آ Or that renownï؟½d mirror of chivalry,
آ آ آ آ Prince Alexander, Philipï؟½s peerless son,
آ آ آ آ Who carried the great war from Macedon
آ آ آ آ Into the Soudanï؟½s realm, and thundered on
آ آ آ آ To die at thirty-five in Babylon.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ What tale did Iseult to the children say,
آ آ آ آ Under the hollies, that bright winterï؟½s day?
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ She told them of the fairy-haunted land
آ آ آ آ Away the other side of Brittany,
آ آ آ آ Beyond the heaths, edged by the lonely sea;
آ آ آ آ Of the deep forest-glades of Broceliande,
آ آ آ آ Through whose green boughs the golden sunshine creeps,
آ آ آ آ Where Merlin by the enchanted thorn-tree sleeps.
آ آ آ آ For here he came with the fay Vivian,
آ آ آ آ One April, when the warm days first began.
آ آ آ آ He was on foot, and that false fay, his friend,
آ آ آ آ On her white palfrey; here he met his end,
آ آ آ آ In these lone sylvan glades, that April-day.
آ آ آ آ This tale of Merlin and the lovely fay
آ آ آ آ Was the one Iseult chose, and she brought clear
آ آ آ آ Before the childrenï؟½s fancy him and her.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ Blowing between the stems, the forest-air
آ آ آ آ Had loosenï؟½d the brown locks of Vivianï؟½s hair,
آ آ آ آ Which playï؟½d on her flushï؟½d cheek, and her blue eyes
آ آ آ آ Sparkled with mocking glee and exercise.
آ آ آ آ Her palfreyï؟½s flanks were mired and bathed in sweat,
آ آ آ آ For they had travellï؟½d far and not stoppï؟½d yet.
آ آ آ آ A brier in that tangled wilderness
آ آ آ آ Had scored her white right hand, which she allows
آ آ آ آ To rest ungloved on her green riding-dress;
آ آ آ آ The other warded off the drooping boughs.
آ آ آ آ But still she chatted on, with her blue eyes
آ آ آ آ Fixï؟½d full on Merlinï؟½s face, her stately prize.
آ آ آ آ Her ï؟½haviour had the morningï؟½s fresh clear grace,
آ آ آ آ The spirit of the woods was in her face.
آ آ آ آ She lookï؟½d so witching fair, that learned wight
آ آ آ آ Forgot his craft, and his best wits took flight;
آ آ آ آ And he grew fond, and eager to obey
آ آ آ آ His mistress, use her empire as she may.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ They came to where the brushwood ceased, and day
آ آ آ آ Peerï؟½d ï؟½twixt the stems; and the ground broke away,
آ آ آ آ In a sloped sward down to a brawling brook;
آ آ آ آ And up as high as where they stood to look
آ آ آ آ On the brookï؟½s farther side was clear, but then
آ آ آ آ The underwood and trees began again.
آ آ آ آ This open glen was studded thick with thorns
آ آ آ آ Then white with blossom; and you saw the horns,
آ آ آ آ Through last yearï؟½s fern, of the shy fallow-deer
آ آ آ آ Who come at noon down to the water here.
آ آ آ آ You saw the bright-eyed squirrels dart along
آ آ آ آ Under the thorns on the green sward; and strong
آ آ آ آ The blackbird whistled from the dingles near,
آ آ آ آ And the weird chipping of the woodpecker
آ آ آ آ Rang lonelily and sharp; the sky was fair,
آ آ آ آ And a fresh breath of spring stirrï؟½d everywhere.
آ آ آ آ Merlin and Vivian stoppï؟½d on the slopeï؟½s brow,
آ آ آ آ To gaze on the light sea of leaf and bough
آ آ آ آ Which glistering plays all round them, lone and mild,
آ آ آ آ As if to itself the quiet forest smiled.
آ آ آ آ Upon the brow-top grew a thorn, and here
آ آ آ آ The grass was dry and mossï؟½d, and you saw clear
آ آ آ آ Across the hollow; white anemonies
آ آ آ آ Starrï؟½d the cool turf, and clumps of primroses
آ آ آ آ Ran out from the dark underwood behind.
آ آ آ آ No fairer resting-place a man could find.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Here let us halt,ï؟½ said Merlin then; and she
آ آ آ آ Nodded, and tied her palfrey to a tree.
آ آ آ آ 
آ آ آ آ They sate them down together, and a sleep
آ آ آ آ Fell upon Merlin, more like death, so deep.
آ آ آ آ Her finger on her lips, then Vivian rose,
آ آ آ آ And from her brown-lockï؟½d head the wimple throws,
آ آ آ آ And takes it in her hand, and waves it over
آ آ آ آ The blossomï؟½d thorn-tree and her sleeping lover.
آ آ آ آ Nine times she waved the fluttering wimple round,
آ آ آ آ And made a little plot of magic ground.
آ آ آ آ And in that daisied circle, as men say,
آ آ آ آ Is Merlin prisoner till the judgment-day;
آ آ آ آ But she herself whither she will can rove,
آ آ آ آ For she was passing weary of his love.
meaning:
About: Matthew Arnold (24 December 1822 ï؟½ 15 April 1888) was an English poet and cultural critic, who worked as an inspector of schools. 
He was the son of Thomas Arnold, the famed headmaster of Rugby School, and brother to Tom Arnold, literary professor, and William Delafield Arnold, novelist and colonial administrator.
